formulas.name: Tone
formulas.formula: The tone of a text is the author's attitude towards the subject.
formulas.notes: The tone is often conveyed through language, imagery, and syntax.

formulas.name: Irony
formulas.formula: A statement or situation that contradicts what is expected or apparent.
formulas.notes: Irony can be verbal, situational, or dramatic.

formulas.name: Symbolism
formulas.formula: A symbol is an object, color, or image that represents an abstract idea or concept.
formulas.variables: Object or image, abstract idea or concept
formulas.notes: Symbols can be literal or abstract, and may have multiple meanings.

formulas.name: Allusion
formulas.formula: A reference to a person, place, event, or work of art that is outside the text itself.
formulas.variables: Person, place, event, or work of art, reference
formulas.notes: Allusions can be used to create meaning, evoke emotions, or add depth to a text.

formulas.name: Foreshadowing
formulas.formula: A hint or clue that suggests an event or plot twist will occur later in the text.
formulas.variables: Event or plot twist, hint or clue
formulas.notes: Foreshadowing can be used to create suspense, build tension, or add complexity to a text.

formulas.name: Author's Purpose
formulas.formula: The reason why the author wrote the text.
formulas.notes: The author's purpose can be to entertain, inform, persuade, or educate the reader.

formulas.name: Literary Devices
formulas.formula: A range of techniques used by authors to create meaning, convey emotions, and engage the reader.
formulas.notes: Literary devices include tone, irony, symbolism, allusion, foreshadowing, and author's purpose.
